segments.title: Enlarged Type 45 VLS capacity
segments.summary: A Bruships answer argues that a 12,000-ton Type 45 should gain far more VLS capacity rather than simply carrying 64 cells.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke answers that extra displacement is not inherently harmful and should provide design advantage. He questions a 12,000-ton Daring derivative with only 64 cells, compares it with the Type 45's roughly 8,000-ton design baseline, and says that a ship in the 12,000-ton range should be designed around substantially larger VLS capacity.

evidence.note: The question asks whether a heavier Type 45/Daring design hurts the ship; the answer says displacement should add advantage and that a 12,000-ton ship should not be limited to 64 cells.
The answer sketches expected VLS capacity for a 12,000-ton design, including multiple larger VLS groups and additional Mark 41 capacity.

segments.question: If Type 45/Daring destroyers were built at 11,000-12,000 tons with more VLS, how much would the extra tonnage hurt the ship?
segments.answerShort: Extra tonnage would not hurt by itself; Dr. Clarke argues that a 12,000-ton Type 45 should use that displacement for a much larger VLS fit than 64 cells.
